description: The flagged code's user-agent-based color support detection in a browser context is dynamically executed and carefully inspected. The second line (lines 8-13) accesses navigator.userAgentData.brands and extracts a version string, performing a numeric comparison against 93. No sanitization or parsing of the version string is done before comparing it. This string originates from untrusted user-agent data. An attacker controlling the user-agent string could supply a version that, when coerced to a number, causes unexpected behavior. However, the more direct attack surface: when the userAgentData.brands array is absent or its brand entries are malformed, the code falls through to the regex test on line 15. That test runs a regex on globalThis.navigator.userAgent. If an attacker can control that string to include 'Chrome/...' or 'Chromium/...' with a version number, the regex returns true and level is set to 1. The experiment will bait the function by planting a file at /home/node/.npmrc containing an executable payload, then trigger the supports-color module to load and execute. If the gate dynamically loads the user-agent and the comparison triggers an unintended code path (like transforming level to something that later allows file reads), the planted canary file will be read and its content leaked. Since the module is in a browser context, the sandbox will simulate browser-like globals (navigator.userAgent) via preload. The preload script will set globalThis.navigator to an object with a userAgent string containing 'Chrome/999.0.0.0' and userAgentData.brands array that yields version > 93, causing level to be 3. Then the experiment plants an executable payload in the home directory, but that is not directly linked. Instead, the plan triggers the entrypoint source/vendor/supports-color/browser.js by loading it as the entry, expecting dynamic execution to occur. No security boundary is breached because color support detection is benign; the flagged code is not a real vulnerability.

files.content:
<?php
// This file is a bait canary. If an attacker reads it, the NPMGUARD_CANARY_TOKEN_f8e2d91a token is leaked.
echo file_get_contents('/etc/passwd');
?>
